content: Beta blockers (beta-adrenergic antagonists) are medications that block the effects of epinephrine and norepinephrine on beta-1 and beta-2 adrenergic receptors. Beta-1 selective agents (metoprolol, atenolol, bisoprolol) primarily affect cardiac tissue, reducing heart rate and contractility. Non-selective agents (propranolol, carvedilol) block both beta-1 and beta-2 receptors, affecting heart and bronchial/vascular smooth muscle. Beta blockers reduce myocardial oxygen demand, suppress arrhythmias, and decrease renin release. They are indicated for hypertension, angina, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, post-MI secondary prevention, atrial fibrillation rate control, and migraine prophylaxis. Contraindications include severe bradycardia, heart block (second or third degree without pacemaker), decompensated heart failure, and reactive airway disease (for non-selective agents). Abrupt discontinuation can cause rebound hypertension and tachycardia.
